1.texto_original: Now when all this was finished, all Israel that were present went out to the cities of Judah, and brake in pieces the pillars, and hewed down the Asherim, and brake down the high places and the altars out of all Judah and Benjamin, in Ephraim also and Manasseh, until they had destroyed them all. Then all the children of Israel returned, every man to his possession, into their own cities.
1.normal:
Devotional Study of 2 Chronicles 31:1

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Now when all this was finished, all Israel that were present went out to the cities of Judah, and brake in pieces the pillars, and hewed down the Asherim, and brake down the high places and the altars out of all Judah and Benjamin, in Ephraim also and Manasseh, until they had destroyed them all. Then all the children of Israel returned, every man to his possession, into their own cities."
1.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of 2 Chronicles 31:1

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Now when all this was finished, all Israel that were present went out to the cities of Judah, and brake in pieces the pillars, and hewed down the Asherim, and brake down the high places and the altars out of all Judah and Benjamin, in Ephraim also and Manasseh, until they had destroyed them all. Then all the children of Israel returned, every man to his possession, into their own cities."
1.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"Now when all this was finished, all Israel that were present went out to the cities of Judah, and brake in pieces the pillars, and hewed down the Asherim, and brake down the high places and the altars out of all Judah and Benjamin, in Ephraim also and Manasseh, until they had destroyed them all. Then all the children of Israel returned, every man to his possession, into their own cities."

2.texto_original: And Hezekiah appointed the courses of the priests and the Levites after their courses, every man according to his service, both the priests and the Levites, for burnt-offerings and for peace-offerings, to minister, and to give thanks, and to praise in the gates of the camp of Jehovah.

10.texto_original: And Azariah the chief priest, of the house of Zadok, answered him and said, Since [the people] began to bring the oblations into the house of Jehovah, we have eaten and had enough, and have left plenty: for Jehovah hath blessed his people; and that which is left is this great store.
10.normal:
Devotional Study of 2 Chronicles 31:10

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"And Azariah the chief priest, of the house of Zadok, answered him and said, Since [the people] began to bring the oblations into the house of Jehovah, we have eaten and had enough, and have left plenty: for Jehovah hath blessed his people; and that which is left is this great store."
10.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of 2 Chronicles 31:10

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"And Azariah the chief priest, of the house of Zadok, answered him and said, Since [the people] began to bring the oblations into the house of Jehovah, we have eaten and had enough, and have left plenty: for Jehovah hath blessed his people; and that which is left is this great store."
10.ninos:
🌌 The Great Beginning! 🌍

Hello, universe explorers! 🚀 Imagine: before there was anything... not even time! ⏰... God decided to CREATE EVERYTHING! ✨

With a powerful "LET THERE BE!" 🤓, God made appear from absolute nothing ✨ the blue skies full of twinkling stars ⭐ and the Earth with mountains 🏔️, rivers 🌊 and animals 🐶. Like the greatest magic ever! 🪄

God is the most amazing artist 🎨 and everything is His masterpiece. Including YOU! 😊 When you see the clouds ☁️ or play in the park 🌳, remember: it all started with Gods creative love! ❤️

"And Azariah the chief priest, of the house of Zadok, answered him and said, Since [the people] began to bring the oblations into the house of Jehovah, we have eaten and had enough, and have left plenty: for Jehovah hath blessed his people; and that which is left is this great store."

"and them that were reckoned by genealogy of all their little ones, their wives, and their sons, and their daughters, through all the congregation: for in their office of trust they sanctified themselves in holiness."

19.texto_original: Also for the sons of Aaron the priests, that were in the fields of the suburbs of their cities, in every city, there were men that were mentioned by name, to give portions to all the males among the priests, and to all that were reckoned by genealogy among the Levites.
19.normal:
Devotional Study of 2 Chronicles 31:19

The first verse establishes the foundation of all biblical revelation: God as sovereign Creator. The phrase "In the beginning" places us at the inaugural moment of creation, not as an event within time, but as the beginning of time itself. God is not part of creation, but its absolute origin.

The verb "created" (from the Hebrew bara) is exclusive to God in the Scriptures, indicating creation ex nihilo (from nothing). This is not about transforming pre-existing matter, but calling into existence what did not exist before. The expression "the heavens and the earth" encompasses the totality of created reality.

This verse answers fundamental questions: Where do we come from? Who is in control? The answer is clear: everything comes from God and depends on Him. As believers, this truth gives us security in divine sovereignty over our lives.

"Also for the sons of Aaron the priests, that were in the fields of the suburbs of their cities, in every city, there were men that were mentioned by name, to give portions to all the males among the priests, and to all that were reckoned by genealogy among the Levites."
19.academico:
Exegetical Analysis of 2 Chronicles 31:19

Philological Analysis

The original Hebrew text presents crucial elements for exegesis. Bereshit (בְּרֵאשִׁית) is a prepositional construction meaning "in the beginning of", indicating the absolute beginning. The verb bara (בָּרָא) appears in perfect tense (qatal), denoting completed action. This verb has God as its exclusive subject in the Tanakh, distinguishing it from asah (to make) and yatsar (to form).

Historical Context

Source criticism identifies this opening with the Priestly tradition (P), dated to the exilic or post-exilic period (6th-5th century BCE). This contrasts with Mesopotamian creation narratives such as the Enuma Elish (18th century BCE), where creation results from divine conflict.

Theological Implications

The theology of creation establishes: (1) Radical monotheism; (2) Creation ex nihilo; (3) Intrinsic goodness of creation; (4) Absolute sovereignty without cosmic opposition.

Archaeological Evidence

Discoveries at Ugarit (Ras Shamra) and Ebla illuminate Canaanite cosmogonic language, showing how Genesis employs common terms but strips them of mythological connotations.

"Also for the sons of Aaron the priests, that were in the fields of the suburbs of their cities, in every city, there were men that were mentioned by name, to give portions to all the males among the priests, and to all that were reckoned by genealogy among the Levites."
